email.subject: The woman Allah heard from above the seven heavens
email.alt_subject: When you are afraid no one is listening
email.preview: As-Sami, the All-Hearing. A hearing that misses nothing, and the prayer that never gets lost on the way up.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a fear that lives quietly underneath a lot of our prayers, and most of us never say it out loud. It is the fear that no one is listening. That the words leave your lips in an empty room and fall to the floor. That the cry you made at two in the morning, with the lights off and no one awake, went nowhere.

One of the names of Allah answers that fear directly: As-Sami, the All-Hearing.

What does it mean that Allah hears? With us, hearing has limits at every edge: the sound must be near enough, loud enough, in a language we know, and we still catch only one voice at a time. Lift all of that away and you begin to approach this name. Commenting on it, the scholar al-Sa'di writes that Allah is the Hearing of all sounds, in their differing languages, across the endless variety of their needs. And Ibn Kathir adds that His hearing encompasses both the one who hides his words and the one who declares them. The thought you never spoke is as audible to Him as an announcement from a rooftop.

If you want to feel it, sit with the woman a whole chapter of the Qur'an is named after. Ibn Kathir relates that Khawla bint Thalaba came to the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, pleading about her husband who had wronged her, and she turned her complaint to Allah. Aisha was in a corner of the very same room, and she said she could not catch some of what Khawla was saying. And from above the seven heavens, Allah had heard every word: 'Certainly has Allah heard the speech of the one who argues with you concerning her husband' (Al-Mujadila 58:1). Years later, Umar stopped his entire procession for this same woman, and when asked why, he said: do you know who this is? This is a woman whose complaint Allah heard from above the seven heavens.

Reading across the Qur'an, this name carries two kinds of hearing. There is the hearing that nothing escapes. And there is the hearing of response, where to hear is to answer, which is why Zakariyya and Ibrahim, given children against every odd, both called Allah 'the Hearer of supplication.' And to Musa and his brother Harun, afraid before the tyrant Pharaoh, Allah said simply: 'Fear not. I am with you both; I hear and I see' (Ta-Ha 20:46). The room did not get easier. They just knew they would not enter it unheard.

So here is the invitation of this name. You do not need volume, you do not need the right words, you do not even need to make a sound. Say the heavy thing, quietly, to the One already listening. Your small voice is not lost in the ocean of voices, because to the All-Hearing there is no ocean of voices. There is only your voice, heard whole, as if you were the only one speaking.

Ya Sami, hear our prayers as You heard those before us, and accept them from us.

If you would like the full reflection, we have written it here:

name: Al-Khafid

names.en: The Abaser, The One who brings low
The Exalter, The One who raises

title: Al-Khafid: The Abaser (with Ar-Rafi, The Exalter)
meta_description: Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The Day that lowers some and raises others, the arrogant who boasted brought down, the plot that perishes. Grounded in khafidatun rafi'a (56:3) and the Names literature. Always taught with Ar-Rafi. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Al-Khafid, Al-Khaafid, Ar-Rafi, The Abaser, The One who brings low, The Exalter, names of Allah, 99 names, Asma ul Husna, khafidatun rafia, Surah Al-Waqia 56:3, Allah lowers the arrogant, izzah belongs to Allah, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, al-Tabari, al-Qurtubi, and Ibn 'Ashur's al-Tahrir wa-l-Tanwir), in the voice of Buruja. Note on the name-form: the definite name Al-Khafid does not appear in the Qur'an; it is grounded in the verbal form khafidatun (56:3) and in the Names literature, as explained in the opening section. Al-Qurtubi states plainly that the true Khafid and Rafi is Allah alone.
lede: We live in a world that looks upside down. The arrogant are on top. The ones who lie loudest, who push to the front, who step on whoever is beneath them, seem to rise and rise, while the quiet and the wronged and the honest stay low. And a question forms in you that you may be ashamed to say out loud: does anyone ever set this right? Is the order of things really the way it looks from down here?

This name answers that, and it does not answer it gently. Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, who abases, who pulls the high down to where they belong. The tradition almost never lets you hold this name alone, though. It hands it to you joined to its companion, Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, the One who lifts the lowly up. He lowers and He raises, and these are not two moods of God at war with each other. They are one perfectly just Hand, and the Day is coming when that Hand will turn the whole upside-down world the right way up, in front of everyone.

sections.h2: A name the Qur'an gives as the Day's own work

ayah.arabic: خَافِضَةٌ رَّافِعَةٌ
ayah.text: It will bring down [some] and raise up [others].

paras: Let us be honest with you from the first line, because this name deserves it. Unlike many of the names in this series, you will not find the word Al-Khafid sitting in the Qur'an in its definite form, the way you find As-Sami or Al-Aziz. What the Qur'an gives you instead is the living word in action. In Surah Al-Waqi'a, describing the Day of Resurrection when it finally falls, Allah says of that Day: khafidatun rafi'a, it brings down and it raises up. Khafidah, lowering. Rafi'ah, raising. The two are spoken in a single breath, two words side by side, and the whole reversal of creation is folded into them.

The name Al-Khafid, then, is how the scholars of the tradition gathered that word into a name, and they did not do it lightly. Al-Qurtubi, commenting on this very verse, says it directly: khafd and rafa', lowering and raising, the Arabs use these words for place and for rank, for honor and for humiliation. And then he settles the matter of the name itself in one sentence worth holding onto: wa al-Khafid wa al-Rafi 'ala al-haqiqa innama huwa Allah wahdah, and the One who truly lowers and the One who truly raises is Allah alone, who raised His friends to the highest of degrees and lowered His enemies to the lowest of depths. The Day does the lowering in the verse, but al-Qurtubi reminds you Who is really behind the Day.

So when we call Allah Al-Khafid, we are not pinning a label on Him that He did not give. We are taking the word He used of the great Day He is bringing, khafidah, and holding it the way the tradition has always held it: as a true name of God, grounded in His own word, even though the definite form Al-Khafid is the tradition's way of carrying it rather than a single Qur'anic title. We wanted you to know exactly where this name stands before we ask you to lean on it. And notice already, in the verse itself, that lowering never comes alone. In the same breath as khafidah is rafi'ah. He does not only bring down. He raises, in the very same act.

sections.h2: Who exactly gets brought low
paras: The Qur'an leaves the Day's lowering and raising as two bare words, khafidatun rafi'a, and the early scholars rushed to fill in who it is that gets lowered and who gets lifted. Read their answers together and a clear picture forms. Ibn Kathir sums it up: that Day lowers peoples down to the lowest of the low, to the Hellfire, even though they were mighty and honored in this world, wa in kanu fi al-dunya a'izza, and it raises others up to the highest heights, to lasting bliss, even though they were lowly in this world, wa in kanu fi al-dunya wuda'a. Sit on that reversal. The exact people the world placed at the top are at the bottom, and the ones the world stepped over are raised highest.

The reports Ibn Kathir, al-Tabari, and al-Qurtubi carry name the lowered ones precisely, and they are exactly who you suspected. As-Suddi: khafadat al-mutakabbirin wa rafa'at al-mutawadi'in, it lowered the arrogant and raised the humble. 'Uthman ibn Saraqa, a cousin of Umar ibn al-Khattab: the Hour lowered the enemies of Allah into the Fire and raised the friends of Allah into the Garden. Muhammad ibn Ka'b: it lowers men who in this world were elevated, and raises men who in this world were brought down. Ibn 'Ata adds a beautiful pair: it lowered some bi-l-'adl, by justice, and raised others bi-l-fadl, by grace. Nothing arbitrary is happening. The arrogant are met with justice. The lowly are met with grace.

Ibn 'Ashur, the great rhetorician, reads the verse the same way and names the lowered ones without flinching: al-jababira wa al-mufsidin, the tyrants and the corruptors, who were in this world in elevation and lordship, are brought down, while the righteous, whom most people did not even bother to notice, are raised. He also points out something the Arabic is doing on purpose. Khafidah and rafi'ah set two opposites against each other in one phrase, a figure the rhetoricians call tibaq, antithesis, both extremes affirmed of the single Day. The verse is built to make you feel the whole world flipping in two words.

sections.h2: The boast that history answered

ayah.arabic: يَقُولُونَ لَئِن رَّجَعْنَا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَّ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَٰكِنَّ الْمُنَافِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
ayah.text: They say, "If we return to al-Madinah, the more honored [for power] will surely expel therefrom the more humble." And to Allah belongs [all] honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know.

paras: You do not have to wait for the Last Day to see Al-Khafid at work. The Qur'an shows you the lowering of the arrogant happening inside history, and there is no clearer scene than this one. Al-Sa'di tells the story: it was on a military expedition, and some friction had broken out between a few of the Muhajirun and the Ansar, and in that moment the hypocrisy of the hypocrites surfaced and they said what was really in their hearts. Their leader, Abdullah ibn Ubayy, sneered that when they got back to Madina, the mightier party, meaning himself and his faction, would drive out the lowlier party, meaning the Prophet and the believers, by his reckoning.

He had the whole thing exactly backwards, and Allah turned it over in the next breath of the verse. Al-Sa'di spells it out: the matter is the reverse of what this hypocrite said, which is why Allah declared, and to Allah belongs all honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers. They are the truly honored ones, al-a'izza, and the hypocrites and their fellow disbelievers are the truly debased ones, al-adhilla. The man who called himself al-a'azz, the mightier, was in reality the lower. The believers he sneered at as al-adhall, the lowlier, were in reality the higher. Al-Khafid had quietly swapped the labels he was so sure of.

And then al-Sa'di names the saddest part: wa lakinna al-munafiqin la ya'lamun, but the hypocrites do not know it. They went on believing they were the mighty ones, deceived by the falsehood they stood on, ightiraran bi-ma hum 'alayhi min al-batil. That is the quiet horror inside being brought low by Allah. It is not only that the arrogant are lowered. It is that they are often the last to know, still feeling tall while the ground has already opened beneath them. We might reflect that this is the most frightening way to fall: certain you are rising, the whole time you are sinking.

sections.h2: Real honor is in one Hand only

ayah.arabic: مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ ۚ وَالَّذِينَ يَمْكُرُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۖ وَمَكْرُ أُولَٰئِكَ هُوَ يَبُورُ
ayah.text: Whoever desires honor [through power] - then to Allah belongs all honor. To Him ascends good speech, and righteous work raises it. But they who plot evil deeds will have a severe punishment, and the plotting of those - it will perish.

paras: If the arrogant are the ones brought low, where is a person supposed to go to actually rise? This verse answers it, and the answer reroutes your whole sense of ambition. Whoever wants 'izzah, real honor, weight, standing, then know that all of it belongs to Allah. Al-Sa'di puts the lesson bluntly: O you who want honor, seek it from the One in whose Hand it lies, for honor is in Allah's Hand and is not attained except by His obedience. You cannot grab it by climbing over people. It is not on the shelf you are reaching for. It is given, by Him, on His terms.

And the verse shows you the only thing that genuinely rises. To Him ascends the good word, and the righteous deed lifts it. Al-Sa'di explains that the good word, every recitation and tasbih and praise and good clean speech, is raised up to Allah and presented to Him, and Allah praises its author before the highest assembly. The righteous deed is what carries it up. This is the true rafa', the real raising, and it is available to the lowest, quietest, most overlooked believer on earth. You raise yourself not by being seen by people but by sending good words and good deeds up to the One who raises.

Now watch what happens to the schemer in the same verse. Those who plot evil have a severe punishment, and their plot, huwa yabur, it perishes. Al-Sa'di draws the contrast exactly along the lines of our two names: as for the evil deeds, they are the reverse, their doer wants to rise by them, and he plots and schemes, and it all comes back on him, and he gains nothing but more humiliation and more sinking, la yazdadu illa ihanatan wa nuzula. Read that slowly. The plotting that was meant to lift him is the very thing that lowers him. He engineered his own descent. Al-Khafid does not always need to push the arrogant down. Often He simply lets their schemes run, and the schemes do the lowering for Him.

sections.h2: Lifted high, then dropped to the lowest

ayah.arabic: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ
ayah.text: We have certainly created man in the best of stature;

ayah.arabic: ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَاهُ أَسْفَلَ سَافِلِينَ
ayah.text: Then We return him to the lowest of the low,

ayah.arabic: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ
ayah.text: Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds, for they will have a reward uninterrupted.

paras: There is a verse that puts the whole drama of khafd and rafa' onto every single human being, and it should make you check which way you are facing. Allah says He created the human being fi ahsani taqwim, in the most beautiful, most upright stature, lifted in form and faculty above the rest of creation. That is where every one of us begins: raised, honored, given a height. And then comes the turn: thumma radadnahu asfala safilin, then We returned him to the lowest of the low. The same phrase, asfal safilin, that Ibn Kathir used for where the arrogant land on the Last Day.

Ibn Kathir, commenting here, says asfal safilin means to the Fire, after that beauty and that fine stature, if he does not obey Allah and follow the messengers. This is the most sobering shape this name takes. The lowering is not only something that happens to distant tyrants. Every human is handed a height at the start, and what we do with it decides whether we keep rising or are dropped to the bottom. The gift of being lifted is not the same as the safety of staying lifted.

But do not miss the exception, because it is the whole hope of the surah: illa alladhina amanu wa 'amilu al-salihat, except those who believe and do righteous deeds, for them is a reward without end. Here the two names split the human race in two. Faith and good action are what keep you in the company of Ar-Rafi, raised, while the descent to asfal safilin is what waits for the one who wastes the height he was given. The fence between being raised forever and being lowered forever is exactly the iman and the 'amal salih the verse names. You are not passive in this. You are choosing, every day, which of His two hands you are walking into.

sections.h2: The companion name: the One who raises

ayah.arabic: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلَاقِ
ayah.text: [He is] the Exalted above [all] degrees, Owner of the Throne; He places the inspiration of His command [i.e., revelation] upon whom He wills of His servants to warn of the Day of Meeting.

paras: We have kept Ar-Rafi walking beside Al-Khafid this whole way, because the tradition will not separate them, and now it is time to look straight at the raising. Al-Khafid is never a God who only takes people down. The same Day that lowers the arrogant raises the friends of Allah to lasting bliss, in the very same verse. And the Qur'an names the raising as one of His attributes: Rafi' al-darajat, the One who is high above all degrees, and the One who raises others through them. Al-Sa'di explains the phrase as al-Ali al-A'la, the High, the Most High, whose own rank is raised infinitely above His creation, and who lifts the ranks of His servants and draws them near and places them above His other creatures.

And He tells you exactly how He raises. Al-Sa'di, on this same verse, says the thing that lifts a servant's degrees is the pure, purifying deed, which is sincerity, ikhlas, the deed done for His face alone, alladhi yarfa'u darajat ashabihi wa yuqarribuhum ilayhi. There is the answer to every lowly believer's quiet ache to matter. You rise not by being loud, not by climbing over anyone, but by sincerity, which the most invisible person on earth can have in full. The Hand that lowers the arrogant is the very same Hand reaching down to lift the sincere.

Hold the two names together and they steady you completely. If you only had Al-Khafid, you might live braced and afraid, as if God's nature were to bring everyone down. If you only had Ar-Rafi, you might forget that arrogance is ever answered at all. The pair tells the truth: the same wise, just Hand lowers the one who made himself high on falsehood and raises the one who made himself low before his Lord. He is not sometimes cruel and sometimes kind. He is always just, and justice looks like lowering in one direction and raising in the other, at the very same moment.

sections.h2: How to live under these two names

ayah.arabic: وَإِذَا قِيلَ انشُزُوا فَانشُزُوا يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ ۚ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ
ayah.text: And when you are told, "Arise," then arise; Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees. And Allah is Aware of what you do.

paras: A name of Allah is never only information. Al-Khafid and Ar-Rafi are meant to reshape you, and they do it in at least three ways.

First, they kill arrogance at the root. Once you truly believe that the One who brings low is watching, you cannot keep making yourself big at the expense of others, because you have seen what He does to exactly that. The mutakabbirun, as-Suddi said, are the ones He lowers. Abdullah ibn Ubayy called himself the mightier and was named the lowest. So you stop reaching for height by stepping on people. You remember that whoever wants honor must seek it from the only One who owns it, and that the surest way to be brought down is to lift yourself up on the heads of others.

Second, they comfort you when you are the one being stepped on. If you are low right now, overlooked, wronged, watching lesser people pass you, this name is a quiet promise that the arrangement is not final and not even real. The Day is khafidatun rafi'a. Al-Khafid sees the one who made himself tall over you, and Ar-Rafi sees you. Your job is not to claw your way up by their methods. Your job is to send good words and good deeds upward, because the good word ascends and the righteous deed raises it, and to trust the Hand that raised the wuda'a, the lowly ones, to the highest heights.

Third, they tell you how to actually rise. Look at how the Qur'an describes it: arise when you are called to make room and serve, and Allah will raise those who believe and those given knowledge by degrees. Al-Sa'di reads it simply: Allah raises the people of knowledge and faith by degrees, according to what He has singled them out with of knowledge and faith. That is the whole ladder. Not status, not noise, not scheming. Iman, 'ilm, sincere deeds quietly sent up. You lower yourself before Him, and He raises you. That is the only climb that ends anywhere but asfal safilin.

sections.h2: The Day the world turns right way up
paras: Step back and feel the whole shape of it. Al-Khafid is the lowering in a universe that desperately needs lowering, and Ar-Rafi is the raising that always answers it, and both are one perfectly just Hand. The Qur'an gave the two as a single breath, khafidatun rafi'a, and that is the truth of the Day that is coming and of every smaller reckoning along the way: nothing high on falsehood stays high, and nothing low in sincerity stays low.

This is the answer to the upside-down world you started with. Right now the arrogant look like they are winning and the honest look like they are losing, and it can shake you. But you are reading the scene at halftime. Al-Khafid has not finished. The tyrants in elevation, the corruptors in lordship, the schemer certain he is rising, the man who sneered that he was the mightier, are all of them being lowered, some already and not even knowing it, all of them by the Last Day. And the overlooked believer, the wronged one, the quiet servant nobody made room for, is being raised by degrees toward the highest of the high. The whole world is being turned the right way up, and on that Day everyone will see it at once.

So do not envy the high and do not despair at being low. Ask only to be on the right side of these two names. Make yourself small before Allah so that you are never made small by Him. Seek your honor from the only Hand that owns it. And when you watch falsehood tower over truth, remember the two words the Qur'an folded the whole Day into, and let them hold you: khafidatun rafi'a. He brings down, and He raises up, and He is not finished yet.

_Note: reading the Day's lowering and raising in 56:3 onto the present injustices of our own world, and onto the personal experience of being overlooked or wronged, is contemplative reflection drawn from the mufassirun's glosses (the arrogant lowered and the humble raised in as-Suddi, the tyrants and corruptors brought down in Ibn 'Ashur, honor belonging to Allah in al-Sa'di), offered as tadabbur and not as a formal scholarly ruling or consensus._

dua.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ يَا خَافِضُ يَا رَافِعُ، ارْفَعْنِي بِطَاعَتِكَ وَلَا تَخْفِضْنِي بِمَعْصِيَتِكَ
dua.translit: Allahumma ya Khafidu ya Rafi'u, irfa'ni bi-ta'atika wa la takhfidni bi-ma'siyatika
dua.en: O Allah, O Abaser, O Exalter, raise me by Your obedience and do not bring me low by disobedience to You.

lessons.bold: Never make yourself big over people.
lessons.text: As-Suddi said the Day lowers the arrogant (al-mutakabbirin) and raises the humble. Abdullah ibn Ubayy called himself the mightier and Allah named him the lowest. The surest way to be brought down is to lift yourself on the heads of others.

lessons.bold: Seek honor only from the One who owns it.
lessons.text: Al-Sa'di teaches that izzah is in Allah's Hand and is reached only through His obedience (35:10). You cannot grab honor by climbing over anyone. It is given by Ar-Rafi, on His terms, to whoever turns to Him.

lessons.bold: Rise by what quietly ascends.
lessons.text: The good word ascends to Allah and the righteous deed raises it (35:10), and He raises the people of faith and knowledge by degrees (58:11). The most overlooked believer can rise this way. Send good words and deeds up, and leave the raising to Him.

lessons.bold: Take heart when you are the one stepped on.
lessons.text: The Day is khafidatun rafi'a (56:3): it lowers the mighty who were a'izza in the world and raises the lowly who were wuda'a, as Ibn Kathir explains. The arrangement you see now is halftime, not the final score. Ar-Rafi sees you.

lessons.bold: Fear rising on falsehood most of all.
lessons.text: The hypocrites 'do not know' they are the debased ones (63:8), and the schemer's own plot perishes and only sinks him further (35:10). The most dangerous fall is the one you take while certain you are climbing. Build your height on truth, or it is no height at all.

faq.q: What does the name Al-Khafid mean?
faq.a: Al-Khafid (الخافض) means The Abaser or The One who brings low, from the root kh-f-d, whose basic sense is to lower, bring down, or humble. Applied to Allah it means the One who lowers the arrogant, the tyrants, and the people of falsehood, by His perfect justice, casting down whoever raised himself up against the truth. Commenting on Surah Al-Waqi'a 56:3, Ibn Kathir explains that the Day brings some down to the lowest of the low even though they were mighty in the world, and as-Suddi glosses it as lowering the arrogant and raising the humble. It is classically taught with its companion Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, never alone.

faq.q: Is Al-Khafid actually in the Qur'an?
faq.a: Not in the definite name-form (الْخَافِض). What the Qur'an gives is the verbal form: in Surah Al-Waqi'a 56:3, the Day of Resurrection is described as khafidatun rafi'a, 'it brings down [some] and raises up [others].' The root kh-f-d appears only four times in the Qur'an, and the other three are the gentle command to 'lower the wing' of humility and mercy (17:24, 15:88, 26:215), so the sense of abasing rests on 56:3 together with the Names literature. The name itself is established in the classical tradition: al-Qurtubi, commenting on 56:3, states plainly that 'the One who truly lowers and the One who truly raises is Allah alone' (al-Khafid wa al-Rafi 'ala al-haqiqa innama huwa Allah wahdah). So the name rests on the word Allah used of the Day He is bringing, with the definite form carried by the tradition.

faq.q: Why is Al-Khafid always paired with Ar-Rafi?
faq.a: Because the Qur'an pairs them. The verse that grounds both, 56:3, speaks them in a single phrase: khafidatun rafi'a, lowering and raising, in one breath. Ibn 'Ashur notes this is a deliberate antithesis (tibaq), both opposites affirmed of the one Day. Held alone, Al-Khafid could be misread as a God whose nature is only to bring people down, and Ar-Rafi as if arrogance is never answered. Together they teach that the same just Hand lowers the one who made himself high on falsehood and raises the one who humbled himself before his Lord, at the very same moment. The lowering and the raising are two motions of one justice, not two competing natures.

faq.q: If Allah is Al-Khafid, why do arrogant people seem to keep winning?
faq.a: The Qur'an answers that you are reading the scene before it is finished. In Surah Al-Munafiqun 63:8, the hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy boasted that 'the more honored will expel the more humble,' and Allah immediately overturned it: honor belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers. Al-Sa'di notes the chilling detail that 'the hypocrites do not know' they are the debased ones; they feel mighty while already being lowered. And in 35:10, the schemer's own plot 'perishes' and, as al-Sa'di says, only sinks him further. So the apparent victory of the arrogant is temporary and often an illusion even now, and on the Day that is khafidatun rafi'a (56:3) it is completely reversed in front of everyone. (Applying this to a specific injustice you are watching is reflection on the tafsir, not a ruling.)

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We started with an upside-down world, where the arrogant rise and the honest stay low, and this name meets us there without pretending the view does not sting. Al-Khafid is the One who brings low, and the Qur'an gives Him to us in the Day's own work, khafidatun rafi'a, spoken in the same breath as the raising, so we never hold the lowering without the lifting. He brings down the mighty who were a'izza in the world and raises the lowly who were wuda'a, as Ibn Kathir reads it. He overturned the boast of the hypocrite who called himself the mightier and was named the lowest. He lets the schemer's plot perish in his own hands. And the same just Hand that lowers the arrogant is reaching down to raise the sincere by their faith and their quiet deeds. To know Al-Khafid and Ar-Rafi is to stop envying the high and stop despairing at the low, and to ask only to be on the right side of these two names when the world finally turns the right way up.

O Allah, Al-Khafid and Ar-Rafi, the One who brings low and the One who raises up, we have watched falsehood tower over truth and it has shaken us. Help us never to build our height on anything but You. Make us small before You so that we are never made small by You. Raise us by Your obedience, by faith and sincere deeds quietly sent up to You, and do not lower us by our own arrogance or our own schemes. And when we are wronged and overlooked, let us trust that the Day is coming when You will set every high and low thing in its true place. Allahumma ya Khafidu ya Rafi'u, irfa'ni bi-ta'atika wa la takhfidni bi-ma'siyatika.

social.caption:
We live in a world that looks upside down. The arrogant rise, the honest stay low, and you wonder if anyone ever sets it right.

Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, meets you there. One honest note first: this name does not appear in the Qur'an in its definite form. What the Qur'an gives is the Day's own work, 'khafidatun rafi'a,' it brings down some and raises up others (56:3). The name is how the classical tradition gathered that word, and al-Qurtubi states plainly that 'the One who truly lowers and truly raises is Allah alone.'

Notice the Qur'an speaks the lowering and the raising in one breath. The tradition always teaches Al-Khafid beside Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, because the same just Hand drops the arrogant and lifts the lowly. And the scholars name exactly who is brought low: as-Suddi says the arrogant (al-mutakabbirin), Ibn 'Ashur says the tyrants and the corruptors, Ibn Kathir says those who were mighty in the world, cast down even as the lowly are raised highest.

You can watch it happen in history. The hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy sneered that 'the mightier will expel the lowlier' (63:8), certain he was the mighty one and the Prophet ﷺ the lowly. Allah overturned it in the next breath: honor belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers. He called himself highest and was named lowest, and 'the hypocrites do not know' it, still feeling tall while sinking.

So how do you rise? Not by climbing over anyone. 'Whoever wants honor, all honor belongs to Allah' (35:10). The good word ascends to Him and the righteous deed raises it, and He raises the people of faith and knowledge by degrees (58:11). The most overlooked believer can rise this way.

The arrogant look like they are winning, but you are reading the scene at halftime. The world is being turned the right way up.

What would change if you truly believed the high and low you see now are not final? 🤍

email.subject: The boast that history answered
email.alt_subject: The name of Allah for an upside-down world
email.preview: Al-Khafid, the One who brings low. The arrogant who look like they are winning are being read at halftime, and the Day is khafidatun rafi'a.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

We live in a world that looks upside down. The arrogant are on top. The ones who lie loudest, who push to the front, who step on whoever is beneath them, seem to rise and rise, while the quiet and the wronged and the honest stay low. And a question forms that we are sometimes ashamed to say out loud: does anyone ever set this right?

One of the names of Allah answers that, and it does not answer gently: Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, the Abaser.

Let us be honest with you from the start, because this name deserves it. Unlike many of the names we write about, Al-Khafid does not appear in the Qur'an in its definite form. What the Qur'an gives instead is the living word in action. Describing the Day of Resurrection when it finally falls, Allah says of that Day: 'khafidatun rafi'a,' it brings down some and raises up others (Al-Waqi'a 56:3). The name is how the scholars of the tradition gathered that word, and they did not do it lightly. Al-Qurtubi, commenting on this very verse, settles it in one sentence: 'the One who truly lowers and the One who truly raises is Allah alone,' who raised His friends to the highest of degrees and lowered His enemies to the lowest of depths.

Notice what the verse refuses to do. It will not let you hear the lowering on its own. In the same breath comes the raising. The tradition has carried them as a pair ever since, Al-Khafid beside Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, because the same just Hand that brings the arrogant down is the very Hand that lifts the lowly up. And the scholars name exactly who is lowered: as-Suddi says the arrogant, Ibn 'Ashur says the tyrants and the corruptors who were in this world in elevation and lordship, and Ibn Kathir says those who were mighty in the world are cast to the lowest of the low, even as the lowly are raised highest.

You can watch it happen inside history. On a military expedition, the hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy sneered that when they returned to Madina 'the mightier will expel the lowlier,' meaning, al-Sa'di explains, that he and his faction were the mighty and the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, and the believers were the lowly. Allah overturned it in the next breath: 'and to Allah belongs all honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers' (63:8). The man who called himself the mightier was named the lowest. And al-Sa'di notes the saddest detail: 'but the hypocrites do not know' it. They went on feeling mighty while the ground had already opened beneath them. That is the most frightening way to fall, certain you are rising the whole time you are sinking.

So how does a person actually rise? Not by climbing over anyone. 'Whoever wants honor, then to Allah belongs all honor' (35:10), and, al-Sa'di says, it is reached only through His obedience. The verse shows the one thing that genuinely ascends: 'to Him ascends the good word, and the righteous deed raises it.' That is the real raising, and it is available to the lowest, quietest, most overlooked believer alive. Meanwhile the schemer's own plot, the verse says, 'perishes,' and al-Sa'di notes it only sinks him further. Al-Khafid often does not need to push the arrogant down. He lets their schemes run, and the schemes do the lowering.

Here, then, is the comfort of this name. If the arrogant around you look like they are winning, you are reading the scene at halftime. The Day is khafidatun rafi'a, and it is not finished. So make yourself small before Allah, so that you are never made small by Him. Seek your honor from the only Hand that owns it. And when falsehood towers over truth, hold onto the two words the Qur'an folded the whole Day into.

Ya Khafid, ya Rafi, raise us by Your obedience, and do not lower us by our own arrogance.

If you would like the full reflection, we have written it here:

name: At-Tawwab

names.en: The Ever-Relenting, the Acceptor of Repentance

title: At-Tawwab: The Ever-Relenting, the Acceptor of Repentance
meta_description: At-Tawwab, the One who keeps turning back to His servants, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The two turnings of repentance, Adam's first words, and the three who were boycotted fifty nights until the earth closed in. Qur'an verified.
keywords: At-Tawwab, At-Tawwaab, The Acceptor of Repentance, The Ever-Relenting, names of Allah, 99 names, tawba, repentance in Islam, how Allah accepts repentance, Surah At-Tawbah, Ka'b ibn Malik, Adam repentance, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, and al-Tafsir al-Muyassar), in the voice of Buruja.
lede: There is a particular kind of distance a person can feel from God, and it is not the distance of someone who never believed. It is the distance of someone who did believe, and then slipped, and now cannot quite face the One they slipped in front of. You know the prayer you have been putting off. You know the habit you keep meaning to leave. And underneath it sits a quiet suspicion that you have used up your turns, that the door you walked away from has been closed behind you. This name was revealed to break that suspicion.

At-Tawwab, the One who keeps turning back. Not a God who reluctantly forgives once and warns you not to come again, but One whose very name, in the intensive Arabic form, means He turns to the returning servant again and again, however many times they come. And the deeper you read into it, the more astonishing it becomes: the scholars find in this name two turnings, not one. He turns the servant back to Himself first, and then He turns to the servant in acceptance. Your repentance was His mercy before it was your choice.

sections.h2: The name, and the form it is built on

ayah.arabic: أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِ وَيَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقَاتِ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Do they not know that it is Allāh who accepts repentance from His servants and receives charities and that it is Allāh who is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful?

paras: Start with the word itself. At-Tawwab comes from three Arabic letters, ta, waw, ba, the root of tawba. And the root means, at its heart, to turn, to return, to come back. When a servant makes tawba, the picture in the word is of someone turning around, away from where they were walking and back toward the One they walked away from. So tawba is not first about feeling bad. It is about turning back.

Now notice the shape the name is pressed into. It is not the plain form of the verb. At-Tawwab sits on an Arabic pattern the language keeps for something intense, abundant, done again and again, a quality so settled it defines the one who carries it. The same pattern gives you words for a person who is constantly, habitually something rather than occasionally. So At-Tawwab does not mean the One who once accepted a repentance. It means the One whose turning back toward His servants never stops, who keeps relenting, keeps receiving, keeps reopening the door, as many times as it is knocked on.

Hear how al-Sa'di puts it, commenting on this very verse. At-Tawwab, he writes, means abundant in turning back toward those who repent: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated from them many times over. Allah does not grow weary of turning to His servants, al-Sa'di adds, until they themselves grow weary and refuse anything but to flee and bolt from His door. Read that again slowly. The limit on His relenting is never reached from His side. It is only ever reached from ours.

sections.h2: The thing the verse is doing to you
paras: It matters where this name lands in the Qur'an. In Surah At-Tawbah it arrives right after Allah mentions accepting repentance from His servants and receiving their charity, both in the same breath. Ibn Kathir reads the verse as a tahyij, a stirring up, an active encouragement toward the two things that wipe a sin away: turning back, and giving. Each of them, he says, erases sins and scrubs them out.

And he carries a striking image from the Prophet ﷺ to show how seriously Allah takes what you bring back to Him. Allah accepts the charity and takes it in His right hand, the hadith says, and nurtures it for one of you the way one of you raises his foal, until the single morsel becomes like the mountain of Uhud. Ibn Kathir even relays the saying of Ibn Mas'ud that the charity falls into the hand of Allah before it falls into the hand of the beggar. Hold that beside the name. The same God who grows a date into a mountain is the God who receives the small, embarrassed turning of a servant who thought it was too little and too late.

So the verse is not informing you of a divine attribute from a distance. It is reaching for your sleeve. Do you not know, it asks, what He is? He is the One who accepts. Why, then, are you still standing outside?

sections.h2: The two turnings hidden in the name

ayah.arabic: فَتَلَقَّىٰ آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: Here is the part of this name most people never hear, and it changes everything. Go back to the very first repentance in human history, Adam after the slip in the garden. The verse says he received words from his Lord, and then He turned to him. Adam did not invent the words of his own repentance. He received them. They were handed to him.

Al-Sa'di stops on exactly this and draws out the structure. His turning, he says of Allah, is of two kinds: first His granting the servant the success to repent, and then, second, His acceptance of the repentance once its conditions are met. And of His mercy toward them, al-Sa'di continues, is that He granted them the success to repent, and pardoned them, and overlooked. So the movement runs like this. Before you ever turned back to Allah, Allah had already turned toward you, softening your heart, placing the regret in your chest, putting the words on your tongue. Your repentance reaching up is the visible half. The invisible half is His mercy reaching down first to make it possible.

Ibn Kathir fills in what those received words were. He carries the report that they are the famous plea preserved elsewhere in the Qur'an, our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers. And he relays from Mujahid a fuller form of Adam's supplication, sealed with this very name: O Allah, there is no god but You, glory and praise be to You, my Lord, I have wronged myself, so turn to me, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful. Even the words Adam used to ask were a gift from the One he was asking.

_Note: the two-turnings framing here is exactly how al-Sa'di glosses 2:37 (tawfiq first, then qabul), presented as his reading and offered for contemplation, not as a separate scholarly category beyond the tafsir._

sections.h2: He turned to them so that they would turn

ayah.arabic: وَعَلَى الثَّلَاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا حَتَّىٰ إِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَظَنُّوا أَن لَّا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: And [He also forgave] the three who were left alone [i.e., boycotted, regretting their error] to the point that the earth closed in on them in spite of its vastness and their souls confined [i.e., anguished] them and they were certain that there is no refuge from Allāh except in Him. Then He turned to them so they could repent. Indeed, Allāh is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: If you want to feel the two turnings in a single life, sit with the three who were left behind. Ibn Kathir preserves the whole story in the long, agreed-upon report of Ka'b ibn Malik. When the Prophet ﷺ set out for the expedition to Tabuk, Ka'b stayed behind, not out of hypocrisy and not for any real excuse, but through pure procrastination. He kept meaning to prepare and kept telling himself there was time, until the army was gone. As he himself says in the report, he had never been stronger or more able to go than in the very campaign he failed to join.

When the Prophet ﷺ returned, the hypocrites came to him with oaths and excuses, and he accepted their outward words and left their hearts to Allah, more than eighty of them. Then Ka'b came. And here is the hinge of the whole story. He could have offered a smooth excuse too. Instead he said, by Allah, I have no excuse, I was never more free or more able than when I stayed behind. The Prophet ﷺ said, as for this one, he has told the truth, so stand until Allah decides about you.

What followed was the closing in the verse describes. The Prophet ﷺ forbade the Muslims to speak to the three of them, and for fifty nights Ka'b lived inside a silence. He would give salam and watch to see if the Prophet's ﷺ lips even moved to return it. The streets he knew went strange to him. He climbed the wall of his own cousin, Abu Qatada, the most beloved of people to him, and gave him salam, and the man would not answer. A letter even arrived from the king of Ghassan offering him refuge and honour somewhere else, and Ka'b recognised it for what it was, another test, and burned it in the oven. The earth, for all its width, had become too narrow to hold him.

Read how al-Sa'di explains the turn when it finally comes. Then He turned to them, he says, means He permitted their repentance and granted them the success to make it, so that, the verse continues, they would repent, that it would actually occur from them, and then Allah would accept it from them. There it is again, unmistakable. He turned to them so that they would turn. The relief reached Ka'b at dawn on the fiftieth night: a man called out from the top of a mountain at the top of his voice, O Ka'b ibn Malik, rejoice. Ka'b fell down in prostration, knowing relief had come. And when he reached the Prophet ﷺ, his face was shining like a piece of the moon, and he said, rejoice with the best day that has passed over you since your mother bore you.

Do not miss what al-Sa'di lifts out of this. The sign that good is coming and the hardship is lifting, he writes, is that the heart attaches itself completely to Allah and cuts itself off from depending on creation. The very phrase the Qur'an uses is that they were certain there was no refuge from Allah except to Him. There is no fleeing away from Him. There is only fleeing toward Him. The narrowness was not Allah abandoning them. It was Allah herding them home.

sections.h2: What real turning back actually is

ayah.arabic: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَبَيَّنُوا فَأُولَٰئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنَا التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Except for those who repent and correct themselves and make evident [what they concealed]. Those - I will accept their repentance, and I am the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: Because His door never closes, it is easy to imagine tawba is a light thing, a word said quickly and then forgotten. The mufassirun are gentle about Allah's side of it and exact about ours. Commenting on this verse, al-Sa'di spells out what those who repent means: that they return from the sins they were committing, with regret, and a leaving off, and a firm resolve not to go back. And then more: it is not enough to abandon the ugly deed, he says, until you also do the good one, and in the case of one who concealed truth it is not enough until he makes clear what he hid. Turning back is regret, plus stopping, plus the inward decision not to return, plus, where you can, repair.

And then, having set the bar honestly, al-Sa'di lands on the mercy of the name. Whoever brings the cause of tawba, he writes, Allah turns to him, because He is At-Tawwab, the One who keeps returning to His servants with pardon and overlooking after the sin when they repent, and with kindness and blessings after withholding when they come back. Notice the second half of that. The name does not only mean He forgives the past. It means He resumes the giving. The relationship does not limp back wounded. He turns to you with ihsan, with new good, as though you had not left.

There is a tenderness in how al-Sa'di reads it that is easy to walk past. Of His mercy, he says, is that He granted them the success to repent so they repented and returned, then He had mercy on them by accepting it from them, out of gentleness and generosity. Both ends of your repentance, the strength to make it and the acceptance of it once made, are described here as His mercy, not your achievement. You contribute the turning. Even that, He made possible.

sections.h2: The name even the prophets reached for

ayah.arabic: رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Our Lord, and make us Muslims [in submission] to You and from our descendants a Muslim nation [in submission] to You. And show us our rites [of worship] and accept our repentance. Indeed, You are the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: Here is something that should reorder how you think about who needs this name. Picture Ibrahim and his son Ismail, raising the foundations of the Ka'ba, two of the greatest human beings who ever lived, in the middle of an act of pure obedience. And listen to how they seal their prayer: and turn to us, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful.

Al-Sa'di explains why these two, of all people, ask this in this moment. Since the servant, whoever he is, he writes, must inevitably fall into shortcoming and stands in need of repentance, they said, and turn to us, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful. Sit with that. Even at the height of devotion, building the very House people would face in prayer for the rest of time, the prophets did not assume they were above needing to be turned back to. They built their tawba into their worship.

This quietly dismantles a lie the heart loves to tell, that you must first become clean, and then approach. The pattern of the prophets is the reverse. You approach in order to be made clean. Tawba is not the toll you pay before the relationship resumes. It is woven into the relationship the whole way through. The most beloved servants of Allah were also the most constant in turning back to Him, and they wrote that turning straight into their du'a.

sections.h2: Even the way you treat people falls under it

ayah.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اجْتَنِبُوا كَثِيرًا مِّنَ الظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ الظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ ۖ وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا وَلَا يَغْتَب بَّعْضُكُم بَعْضًا ۚ أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَّحِيمٌ
ayah.text: O you who have believed, avoid much [negative] assumption. Indeed, some assumption is sin. And do not spy or backbite each other. Would one of you like to eat the flesh of his brother when dead? You would detest it. And fear Allāh; indeed, Allāh is Accepting of Repentance and Merciful.

paras: We tend to file repentance under the big, private sins, the ones we are ashamed of in the dark. But look where the Qur'an plants this name in Surah Al-Hujurat. It comes at the close of a verse about the everyday sins of the tongue and the heart toward other people: suspicion, spying, backbiting. Allah even gives the unforgettable image, would any of you love to eat the flesh of his dead brother, and then seals it, and fear Allah, indeed Allah is At-Tawwab, the Merciful.

Al-Sa'di reads the placement of the name as both warning and door. The verse, he says, is a severe warning against backbiting, and a sign that backbiting is among the major sins, because Allah likened it to eating the flesh of the dead. And yet the verse does not end on the threat. It ends on the name. He defines it right here: At-Tawwab is the One who permits His servant's repentance and grants him the success for it, then turns to him by accepting it, merciful to His servants in that He called them to what benefits them. So even the sin you commit against your brother, behind his back, with your words, is met at the end of the verse by the open door of the One who keeps turning back.

Al-Muyassar renders the close of the verse simply: indeed Allah is ever-relenting toward His believing servants, merciful to them. There is a mercy in where this name sits. It tells you that the slips of the tongue you barely notice are real, serious enough that Allah names them in His Book. And in the same line it tells you the way back from them is the same as the way back from anything: turn, and you will find Him already turning to you.

sections.h2: Live as someone who can always come back
paras: A name of Allah is never only something to know. It is meant to remake the way you live, and At-Tawwab remakes you in at least three ways.

First, it kills despair at the root. The single most dangerous lie after a sin is that it is too late, that you are too far gone, that you have asked too many times already. Al-Sa'di answered it directly: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times over, and Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until His servants weary of asking. The door is not a one-time door. So the right response to falling is not to lie where you fell, deciding the relationship is over. It is to get up and turn around again, today, knowing the One you are turning to has been waiting, facing you the whole time.

Second, it changes the speed of your return. Look at what nearly destroyed Ka'b ibn Malik and what saved him. What endangered him was delay, putting off the thing he meant to do until the moment passed. What saved him was the truth, told plainly and at once: by Allah, I have no excuse. The pattern for the believer is to make tawba honest and immediate, to refuse the smooth excuse we tell even ourselves, and to come back without the long detour through self-justification.

Third, it makes you gentle with other people who are trying to come back. The God whose name is At-Tawwab keeps reopening His door to the same servants again and again. A heart that has truly tasted that cannot stand at its own little door turning people away, cannot greet someone else's repentance with suspicion, cannot remind a person forever of the sin they have already left. You were received when you thought you were finished. Now you become someone who makes it easy for others to come back, the way it was made easy for you.

sections.h2: The door that faces you
paras: Step back and let the whole shape of it settle. The fear underneath so much of our distance from God is that the door has closed, that we have spent our turns, that the One we let down is now turned away from us. At-Tawwab is the Qur'an's answer to that fear, and it is not a soft reassurance. It is a name of God, built on a form that means again, and again, and again.

And it answers the fear more completely than you dared hope. You were afraid you had to find your own way back. But the scholars show you the way back was His mercy before it was your effort: He turned the three home by closing the earth around them until there was nowhere left to flee but to Him, He handed Adam the very words of his repentance, He turned to them so that they would turn. Your regret, your longing to return, the fact that you are even reading this and feeling the pull, is itself the first half of His turning toward you.

So whatever it is you have been carrying, the prayer you stopped praying, the thing you keep going back to, the distance you assumed was permanent, hear the name and turn around. He is At-Tawwab. He was facing you before you turned. The door you thought was shut was never a door He closed. It was only a back you needed to turn around.

dua.arabic: رَبَّنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
dua.translit: Rabbana wa tub alayna innaka anta at-Tawwab ar-Rahim
dua.en: Our Lord, and accept our repentance. Indeed, You are the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

lessons.bold: Never decide the door is closed.
lessons.text: Al-Sa'di teaches that whoever turns to Allah, He turns to them, even if the sin is repeated many times, and that He never wearies of accepting repentance until the servant wearies of asking. After a fall, do not lie where you fell. Get up and turn around again.

lessons.bold: Let your repentance be His mercy, not your achievement.
lessons.text: On 2:37 al-Sa'di describes two turnings: Allah first grants you the success to repent, then accepts it. The regret in your chest and the words on your tongue are already gifts. Stop waiting to feel worthy, and turn.

lessons.bold: Come back quickly, and come back honest.
lessons.text: Procrastination nearly ruined Ka'b ibn Malik; the plain truth, by Allah I have no excuse, is what saved him. Refuse the smooth excuse you tell even yourself. Make your tawba immediate and truthful.

lessons.bold: Build turning back into your worship.
lessons.text: Even Ibrahim and Ismail, raising the Ka'ba, sealed their prayer with You are At-Tawwab. You do not become clean and then approach. You approach in order to be made clean. Weave istighfar through your good days, not only your worst ones.

lessons.bold: Reopen your own door to others.
lessons.text: The One who keeps receiving you back again and again makes you someone who receives others. Do not meet a person's repentance with suspicion or hold their old sin over them forever. You were received when you thought you were finished.

faq.q: What does the name At-Tawwab mean?
faq.a: At-Tawwab (التواب) means the Acceptor of Repentance, or the Ever-Relenting, from the root t-w-b, which means to turn or return. It is built on an intensive Arabic form, so it does not mean the One who accepted a repentance once, but the One who keeps turning back to His servants again and again. Commenting on Surah At-Tawbah 9:104, al-Sa'di explains it as abundant in turning toward those who repent: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times, and Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until the servant himself wearies of asking.

faq.q: What are the two turnings of repentance?
faq.a: Commenting on Adam's repentance in Al-Baqarah 2:37, al-Sa'di notes that Allah's turning is of two kinds: first He grants the servant the success (tawfiq) to repent, and then He accepts the repentance once its conditions are met. In other words, before you turn back to Allah, Allah has already turned toward you, softening the heart and even, in Adam's case, handing him the very words of his repentance. Your reaching up is the visible half; His mercy reaching down to make it possible is the first half. (This framing is al-Sa'di's reading of the verse, offered for reflection.)

faq.q: Is there a limit to how many times Allah will accept my repentance?
faq.a: According to al-Sa'di's commentary on 9:104, the limit is never reached from Allah's side. He writes that whoever turns to Allah, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times over, and that Allah does not grow weary of turning back to His servants until they grow weary and refuse anything but to flee from His door. So the door does not close after a set number of returns; the only way it closes is if the servant stops coming. (See also 2:37, 2:128, 2:160, and 49:12, where the name recurs.)

faq.q: What does true repentance (tawba) require?
faq.a: On Al-Baqarah 2:160, al-Sa'di lists what 'those who repent' means: returning from the sin with regret, leaving it off, and a firm resolve not to return to it. He adds that it is not enough to abandon the bad deed without doing the good one, and that someone who concealed a truth must also make clear what they hid. So true tawba is regret, stopping, the inward decision not to go back, and, where possible, repair. And al-Sa'di stresses that the strength to do all of this is itself part of Allah's mercy, not the servant's achievement alone.

faq.q: How is At-Tawwab different from Al-Ghaffar, the Ever-Forgiving?
faq.a: The two names are close companions and the Qur'an often pairs the meaning of repentance with forgiveness. The emphasis differs. Al-Ghaffar centres on the forgiveness itself, the covering and wiping away of the sin. At-Tawwab centres on the turning: the movement of Allah back toward the servant, and the servant back toward Allah. As al-Sa'di puts it on 2:160, At-Tawwab keeps returning to His servants with pardon after the sin and with renewed kindness and blessing after withholding, so the name speaks not only to a sin being erased but to the whole relationship being turned back and resumed.

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We carry a quiet fear that we have used up our turns, that the door we walked away from has been shut behind us. At-Tawwab is the answer the Qur'an gives to that fear, not as a comforting feeling but as a name of God, built on a form that means again, and again, and again. He is the One who handed Adam the words of his repentance, who closed the wide earth around three boycotted men for fifty nights until there was nowhere left to flee but to Him, who turned to them so that they would turn, and whom even Ibrahim and Ismail called upon while building His House. The scholars find two turnings in this name: He turns the servant home first, then He turns to the servant in acceptance. Your very longing to come back is already the first half of His mercy. To know this name is to never again believe that it is too late.

O Allah, At-Tawwab, the Ever-Relenting, You turn to Your servants before they turn to You, and You keep turning to them however many times they come. Turn our hearts back to You, grant us the strength to return, and accept it from us when we do. Make our repentance honest and quick, keep us from despairing of Your door, and let us be people who hold our own door open to others the way You held Yours open to us. Rabbana wa tub alayna innaka anta at-Tawwab ar-Rahim.

social.caption:
There is a quiet fear that lives under a lot of our distance from God: that we have used up our turns, that the door is closed.

At-Tawwab, the Acceptor of Repentance, the Ever-Relenting, is the answer the Qur'an gives. Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Muyassar), the name comes from the root t-w-b, to turn back, in an intensive form: the One who keeps turning to His servants again and again. Al-Sa'di says it plainly: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the sin is repeated many times, and Allah never wearies of accepting repentance until the servant wearies of asking.

The most astonishing part is what the scholars call the two turnings. On Adam's repentance (2:37), al-Sa'di explains that Allah first grants the servant the success to repent, and then accepts it. Adam did not invent his words of repentance; he received them from his Lord. Your very longing to come back is already the first half of His mercy.

You see it in the three boycotted after Tabuk: Allah closed the wide earth around them until there was nowhere to flee but to Him, then, in the Qur'an's exact words, 'He turned to them so they could turn' (9:118). And even Ibrahim and Ismail, raising the Ka'ba, sealed their prayer with 'You are At-Tawwab.'

So turn around. He was facing you before you moved. 🤍

email.subject: The door you thought was closed
email.alt_subject: The name built on again, and again, and again
email.preview: At-Tawwab, the One who keeps turning back. The two turnings of repentance, and why your longing to return is already His mercy.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a particular kind of distance from God, and it does not belong to the person who never believed. It belongs to the one who did believe, then slipped, and now cannot quite face the One they slipped in front of. You know the prayer you have been putting off. You know the habit you keep meaning to leave. And underneath sits a quiet suspicion that you have used up your turns, that the door has closed behind you.

One of the names of Allah was revealed to break that suspicion: At-Tawwab, the Acceptor of Repentance, the Ever-Relenting.

The name comes from the root t-w-b, which means to turn, to return. When a servant repents, the picture in the word is of someone turning around, back toward the One they walked away from. And the name sits on an intensive Arabic form, so it does not mean the One who accepted a repentance once. Commenting on Surah At-Tawbah 9:104, the scholar al-Sa'di explains it as the One who keeps turning back to those who repent: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times, and Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until the servant himself wearies of asking. The limit is never reached from His side.

Now here is the part most of us never hear. The scholars find two turnings in this name, not one. Commenting on Adam's repentance (Al-Baqarah 2:37), al-Sa'di writes that Allah's turning is of two kinds: first He grants the servant the success to repent, and then He accepts the repentance once it is made. Look at the verse: Adam received words from his Lord. He did not invent the words of his own repentance. He received them. So before you ever turn back to Allah, Allah has already turned toward you, placing the regret in your chest and the words on your tongue. Your reaching up is the visible half. His mercy reaching down to make it possible is the first half.

You can feel both turnings in one life. Ibn Kathir preserves the long, agreed-upon report of Ka'b ibn Malik, one of the three left behind after the expedition to Tabuk. Ka'b had no excuse; he simply procrastinated until the army was gone. For fifty nights the Muslims were told not to speak to him. He would greet the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, and watch to see if his lips even moved to answer. He climbed the wall of his own beloved cousin, who would not return his salam. The earth, in the Qur'an's words, closed in on him for all its width. And then relief came at dawn: a man called from the top of a mountain, O Ka'b, rejoice. Read the exact words Allah uses for it: 'Then He turned to them so they could repent' (9:118). Al-Sa'di explains: He permitted and enabled their repentance, so that it would happen, and then He would accept it. He turned to them so that they would turn.

And if you think this name is only for the great private sins, look where the Qur'an also plants it: at the end of a verse about suspicion and backbiting (Al-Hujurat 49:12). Even the sins of the tongue against your brother are met by the open door of the One who keeps turning back. And even Ibrahim and Ismail, building the Ka'ba in the middle of pure obedience, sealed their prayer with 'and turn to us, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful' (2:128). The prophets did not become clean and then approach. They approached in order to be made clean.

So whatever you have been carrying, hear the name and turn around. He is At-Tawwab. He was facing you before you turned. The door you thought was shut was never a door He closed.

Ya Tawwab, turn our hearts back to You, and accept it from us when we do.
